feed.title: Reid’s Unzipped
feed.subtitle: Following the Reid family as they sail their catamaran Zipper across the world's oceans — adventures, mishaps, and everything in between.

entry.title: A year in Whangarei?!
entry.content:
An unplanned year in Whangarei! During a year working and at school we’ve dealt with a stroke, skin cancer and now a broken arm — we feel we’ve tested the NZ health service sufficiently, so it’s time to go sailing again. It’s also been a blast, between times!

So, that’s a year then! I think we did at least tell you we were stopping for a bit and wouldn’t be posting any updates, but we had expected to sail back North from New Zealand at the start of May.

So, what happened?!

Well, we’ve had some ups and downs, but through all of that we’ve had an amazing time and all four of us have loved it here! Isla and Angus have now been in Whangarei Primary for more than a year (they started in mid-November last year), and have absolutely excelled. Going from a school of 80 in total at Itchen Abbas to over 500 pupils in the same primary school age bracket here — and a year cooped up with their parents with Grandparent rotations in between — it’s fair to say it’s a completely different experience. They have done what they do and thrown all of themselves into it, and are thriving as a result. The hardest part now is that we’ve been here too long in some respects; they’ve had enough time to really make friends and start to feel more settled. Emotional times as this term comes to an end and we prepare to finally stow the mooring lines and put the anchor back to work.

We’ll try and write a bit more another time, but here’s an abridged version in the meantime…

The Stroke

Today, 27th November, is a date we’ll always remember. One year ago today I had recently started back at work, I was walking back to the boat to meet Sophie for lunch. In the middle of Whangarei, waiting at a pedestrian crossing, I started to feel odd — numbness like a dental block on my left jaw — and relatively quickly deteriorated with a mixture of tingling and numbness. Slowly enough that I could call Sophie, but quickly enough that I knew something was seriously wrong. I was pretty confident that these were the symptoms of a stroke. I sent a pin with my location to Sophie at 13:00, and at 14:00 I was in a CT scanner at the hospital here in Whangarei. That’s not an hour of my life I want to go through again — the most extraordinary part of it was being completely mentally aware of what was happening, but completely losing the ability to control my body. At times I had complete paralysis down my left side, no movement at all in my left arm or leg, and unintelligible garbled speech.

The service to get me from the street into a CT scanner and the care of a specialist stroke consultant within an hour was outstanding. There are not many places in the world where that would happen, so although I might be a little aggrieved to have had a stroke, I was seriously lucky that we had just arrived here. I can’t imagine what it would have been like at sea. Fortunately, my symptoms resolved themselves naturally whilst I was in the scanner; by the time I was back on the ward I was able to talk properly again, and my limbs were behaving when asked to move. This was a relief, to say the least!

After some recovery and a few tests it was confirmed I needed a heart op, which I had in Auckland in April. The hardest part for me had been getting my head around why and how this had happened when I was about as fit and healthy as I’ve ever been.

I’m sure you can see why we chose not to tackle the ocean crossing back up to the tropics in May! Instead, as these types of sailing adventures are dictated by annual hurricane/cyclone seasons, our choices were to sell up and head home, or to add a year. Tempting as it was to come home we really weren’t ready to throw it all in yet. So, with support all round — for which we’ll eternally be grateful — it was school and work through the rest of 2025 so we can get back on the water in 2026.

Sophie’s Turn

Unfortunately, it seems Sophie didn’t want to miss out! Almost immediately after my op she was diagnosed with a skin cancer (fortunately a BCC, so not spread) on the side of her nose. What can often be a relatively simple operation turned into a complicated procedure as it was both deep and conspicuous, with scars extending from her eyebrow down to the side of her mouth. They are extremely vigilant on skin cancers here in NZ as they are sadly very common, and are therefore very experienced in treating them.

The most frustrating thing is that this was a mole Sophie had had for years — not one she’d picked up in our year sailing the tropics as you might have imagined. She’s very careful all the time and had already had this particular mole checked out not once, not twice, but three times back in the UK. Disappointingly, the response she received was pretty much: stop being so vain, it’s just a mole. We can’t help but feel it would have been substantially less invasive to remove a few years ago.

But anyway, it’s off now, Sophie has pretty much rebuilt her muscle movement and is working on improving her sensation further. She has one more trip to the surgeon in Auckland before her “nose job” is complete. A lesson for us all though — if you’re not already, you should learn to be a smiler. Sophie’s smile creases have been perfect for hiding her scars!

Angus Joins the Club

And then, just as we thought we’d tested the New Zealand health service enough, Angus wanted in — breaking his arm at the first cricket practice of the year. Although it was obviously pretty painful at the time, it was fortunately a greenstick and not a bad fracture, so we’re still hoping our plan for a pre-Christmas departure from Whangarei is on. His cast came off this week!

And Isla

Finally, just in case she gets any ideas, we’re considering locking Isla in her cabin until we get out of New Zealand waters. But it wouldn’t be fair if we didn’t put her in here too — the girl keeping the whole show on the road, who turned 10 last week!

So there you have it — a bit of a tumultuous year that we will be pleased to put behind us. But it hasn’t all been bad; we have made some incredible friends and have really enjoyed our stay in Whangarei. It’s time to get back to what we love and start sailing and exploring again.
entry.summary: An unplanned year in Whangarei — dealing with a stroke, skin cancer and a broken arm, we feel we've tested the NZ health service sufficiently. It's time to go sailing again.

entry.title: Cherbourg to Whangarei: 15,577Nm
entry.content:
15,577 miles since leaving France we have arrived in New Zealand…

One thing we’ve learned since setting out from Cherbourg almost exactly a year ago is that regularly posting timely blogs is beyond us — sorry about that!

We have so much more we would like to share, but for now, because we’ve arrived in New Zealand, it’s time for some headlines. It’s kind of cool, and feels totally crazy, to look at our track — we have to zoom out on the whole world to really appreciate it:

Some context to the adventure

  • We’ve sailed 15,577 miles (72% of the earth’s circumference — we are only halfway round as we didn’t go in a straight line!)
  • We’ve visited 18 countries (or major island groups thereof, in French Polynesia and the Caribbean)
  • We reached 15 knots boat speed several times, with a max of 18.2
  • We broke the 200 mile day twice
  • We stopped on passage twice for emergency repairs in a country we hadn’t planned to visit
  • We dragged anchor three times
  • We crashed the boat once (the third drag)
  • We took part in three rescues at sea
  • We’ve met more boats and made more friends than we can remember
  • We’ve swum with more sea life than we could have imagined
  • We’ve had an amazing time…!!

The Arrival

Our arrival into New Zealand was one we couldn’t possibly have imagined. We left Tonga with a crew of five (with Paul Dugdale joining us for the passage) — these things are very official, with important documentation on our exit from the country to show for it. However, having come to the aid of the crew on a stricken yacht on the way over, we entered New Zealand with a crew of eight. Fortunately the rescue was well known by the time we arrived, and all the official bodies from NZ Maritime to customs and marina staff were ready and expecting Zipper — bringing three Kiwis home to NZ with us did no harm to our passage through the official check-in procedures!

We sailed down the Northland coastline on a beautiful morning with a glorious wind for our Code 0. There was no evidence, other than in our exhausted emotions, of the rough seas and frontal winds from the previous couple of days. This was sailing at its best (although a bit cold for us — we’re not in the tropics anymore)!

Whangarei

We’re now moored in the Town Basin marina in Whangarei surrounded by a plethora of amazing cafes, bars, restaurants and supermarkets. This is taking us some getting used to after being so remote for so long — shopping in a supermarket feels overwhelming to say the least. How do you possibly choose what to buy when literally everything is available?!

We have a few more days to relax and unwind as we prepare for getting back into the routines of work and school. For now, Zipper is staying put — we won’t be adding to the mileage for at least a few months…
entry.summary: 15,577 miles since leaving France, we have arrived in New Zealand — and we brought three Kiwis home with us.

entry.title: Makatea – mined, abandoned and reborn
entry.content:
Makatea is a unique world. A small island between Tahiti and the Tuamotus, it is only 4 miles across. The nearest Tuamotu, Rangiroa, lies 40 miles to the North.

But Makatea is not “just another Tuamotu.” Opposing the trend of everything else here, where volcanically formed islands sink to become atolls, this is an atoll that has been pushed up out of the ocean. The coral platform that used to reside on the surface is now nearly 100m up in the sky, bounded by the same sheer cliffs that are a feature of the sharp underwater drop-offs that surround the atolls themselves.

The Past

Coral sand dried out over a million years has become phosphate — and that is what the island was mined for between 1906 and 1966. The phosphate here is very pure, perhaps the highest quality phosphate in the world, and therefore sought after for agriculture by all nations. Exports went all around the world including France, the US, China and Japan.

To set up an operation of 3,000 to 4,000 people on a remote island requires the installation of every facet of life for the workers. They needed a power station, bakery, butchers, cinema, theatre — the island had to become somewhere with facilities for everyday life. So there were jobs for all, not just engineers and miners, and these people were brought to the island too. Apparently at its peak the island had 30 football teams!

The Present

Mining stopped in 1966 and most of the workers left. However when a government or company decides to stop work, there is no incentive to spend any time or money decommissioning — so pretty much everything remains. The industrial machinery, railways and port structure are all sitting here rusting away. It really is extraordinary to see and imagine what it was like — the island has a population of just 60 today!

The landscape is harsh — although relatively flat at scale, the dried coral is sharp and pitted. And the mining pits are not a foot or two deep; some are 50m deep yet barely 5m across. When the miners started digging a new hole the instruction was simple: keep digging until you can’t dig any further.

The sand was loaded into wheelbarrows, pushed to the nearest railroad, and then a funicular dropped the material off the high plateau down to sea level. The foundations of a huge swinging crane still stand on the edge of the reef. On approach we thought at first it looked like a huge shipwreck, or an enormous cannon — but of course it is neither!

The Future

Makatea is being reborn. Since moorings were installed in 2018 this has become a magical place to stop for a self-sufficient cruiser. Few cruising here before then would even have known it existed. There are no restaurants or bars, and only a couple of very small shops supplied by boat once a month.

Tapu, the mayor’s son, takes tourists on half-day island tours — this is what we did, and we can’t recommend it highly enough. He is passionate, knowledgeable, energetic and fun. He has started setting up a rock climbing operation with three sites already and hundreds of routes for all levels — we reckon it has the potential to become a world-class climbing destination.

It’s the kind of place where you wonder whether you’re seeing the “before it got famous” moment — and hope it stays just like this a little longer.
entry.summary: A small island between Tahiti and the Tuamotus, Makatea is unlike anywhere else — pushed up from the ocean, mined for phosphate, and now quietly coming back to life.

entry.title: Video Highlights – Makatea
entry.content:
Some highlights of our stay in Makatea — exploring the island with Tapu.

We took a tour with Tapu, an energetic and passionate guy working hard to bring more tourism to Makatea. Son of the mayor, Julien, Tapu has worked in events in Tahiti for several years before returning to the island. He is working on all sorts of initiatives, including setting up a mountain climbing business (the perfect location for it!) and organising festivals on the island. We loved the day we spent with him being shown around — absolutely recommended!

Makatea Highlights Open in YouTube ↗
entry.summary: Some highlights of our stay in Makatea, exploring the island with the irrepressible Tapu.

entry.title: Arrival in Makatea
entry.content:
Wowsers, what a place this is!!!

We were just starting to feel a bit sad as we approached the island. It is an island — not an atoll — and as we could see it growing in the distance it firmly marked the end of our time in the Tuamotus proper. We know there is a lot more to see as we head further West and we are excited about that, however the Tuamotus really are somewhere special and in some ways perhaps our minds had cast them as the highlight of the whole trip.

But we needn’t have worried. Makatea really is spectacular! The coastline is extraordinary — sheer cliffs with large overhangs cut out by the force of the ocean. As we rounded the Northern shore a coral ledge edged into view, and we had our fingers firmly crossed as we counted the number of masts. There are four buoys here, and no anchoring options. Counting 4 masts we were anxious, however as we got closer it was clear that two were on the same yacht — one was a ketch — so we knew there was one buoy available.

It was only in 2018 that the buoys were installed on the edge of the reef. Prior to that it hadn’t been possible to visit Makatea, so it really is a very recent addition to the cruising route. And what an amazing addition it is!

As we headed up to drop the mainsail Sophie spotted two humpback whales breaching ahead of us, perhaps a little over a mile away. Enormous plumes of spray shot up, so we stayed watching the show for a while as they slapped their pectorals and tails between their occasional breaches. Wow! We had heard whale season had started in the leeward islands, but we weren’t expecting any here. What an incredible welcome!

The Mooring

Now we just needed to find the free buoy. Picking it up really did feel close to the reef — Pacific swell crashing onto the reef a mere couple of boat lengths away. The warp itself didn’t have a metal eye in it and was a little frayed in parts, so we weren’t filled with confidence. After diving in to investigate further, the chain comes all the way up to a couple of metres below the surface — so we put our anchor bridle directly down onto that and felt very secure. Well, as long as the wind doesn’t go West…

The proximity is understandable — just a further boat length offshore and the depth is, as far as our instruments can determine, infinite. The coral wall simply falls into the abyss. The water is crystal clear, this feels very like mooring in Bonaire but with a more extreme drop-off.

Tapu, the mayor’s son whom we had a WhatsApp chat with a few days earlier, came down to the harbour to welcome us. He hailed us on VHF16 and we went into the harbour in the dinghy to meet him. His English is excellent, and with a tour around the island to look forward to tomorrow we are very excited.

The next morning, after moving South to a buoy slightly further from the reef:
entry.summary: We were just starting to feel a bit sad as we left the Tuamotus behind — we needn't have worried. Makatea is spectacular.

entry.title: Enjoying Toau – Anse Amyot
entry.content:
A couple of days in the false pass at Anse Amyot on the NW of Toau. This was our last atoll in the Tuamotus — and it did not disappoint.

After sailing through Fakarava North Pass, we headed for the corner of Toau to sail around to the North West where there is a false pass. This is a protected lagoon, where a wonderful family lives and 10 buoys are laid for cruisers.

After a slightly damp start the clouds dispersed and we had a perfect downwind sail under main and jib, up the side of the atoll and round the top. We were slightly concerned that Anse Amyot would be full, with only 10 buoys and limited anchoring space, so we had begun thinking of contingency plans. On arrival, however, we found the last free mooring buoy — relief!

Gaston and Valentine

After taking up the buoy, we put the dinghy in the water and headed ashore to say hello to Gaston and Valentine. They gave us a warm welcome and invited us to look around — they have lots of animals and the children were particularly taken with the little piglets that came running up as we approached the pig pen.

Valentine speaks wonderful English and told us where to snorkel and dive. We went back the following day and ordered cooked lobster — it was the same price whether Gaston cooked it or not, so we decided to have him cook it on his BBQ. A great decision; the lobster was fantastic!

Spearfishing

Valentine told us that in 60 years they had never had ciguatera, but they ate the parrot fish and never grouper or snapper. They were very happy for us to try and catch our own. Jamie has been eager to try out the spear gun so it seemed an opportunity not to miss. After a few warm-up shots, the hunter gatherer returned to Zipper with parrot fish for supper! They are beautiful fish and unlike others retain their colour when they die. They have a very delicate white flesh and are absolutely delicious.

The Outside Wall

We had a couple of great snorkels and an astonishing dive off the outside wall — a sheer drop off into the abyss. We went to the Yellow Dog dive site recommended by Valentine. It was our first dive together and we both loved it, even if it was a little edgy — half a mile up the coast outside the atoll, in strong current, very much on our own (we did take our handheld VHF just in case!).

Because of the limited mooring balls and the communal area created by Valentine and Gaston, Anse Amyot is a very friendly and sociable place to visit. We met a French family who had spent 15 months cruising four years ago and had decided to charter a catamaran to revisit some of their favourite places. It seems Anse Amyot has a certain charm that draws people back.

We really enjoyed our time here and highly recommend it to anyone in the Tuamotus.
entry.summary: Our last atoll in the Tuamotus, and it did not disappoint. Lobster, spearfishing, diving, and the warmth of Gaston and Valentine's hospitality.

entry.title: Video Highlights – The Tuamotus
entry.content:
What an amazing month we’ve had in the most spectacular coral atolls — the Tuamotus, in the middle of the Pacific!

The Tuamotus are mostly about what’s going on under the water, so “highlights” from above is a bit of a misnomer really. But you’ll get the gist. What’s going on underwater is something else entirely — see our posts on Fakarava, Kauehi and Tahanea for that.

Tuamotus Highlights Open in YouTube ↗
entry.summary: What an amazing month in the most spectacular coral atolls in the middle of the Pacific.

entry.title: Tuamotus Passes
entry.content:
Coming out of the North pass at Fakarava this morning, it felt like time to write down our experience of the various atoll passes before we forget! This one was textbook. Some of our others were far from that — but actually they were all very manageable, at no stage were we at all concerned.

Raroia

Entry: Our first atoll, and our first pass. We aimed to time this one with the early morning slack but missed it by an hour or so. With an outgoing current we expected it would probably be fine — and indeed the conditions were not bad at all. There were standing waves in the middle of the outflow, but we snuck in to the North of all this — a huge advantage of our shallow draft. Smooth water, perhaps 2 knots current, nice and easy entry.

Exit: Again fine, with the same route out on the North side. 3 knots of current swept us out quickly, but the water was smooth and easy to navigate.

Tahanea

Entry: We got to this one about 10 in the morning, a couple of hours before high water slack so we went in on the flood. Flat calm and completely benign conditions, nice and easy.

Exit: Our confidence was up after some easy passages, but this time it was windy. The alarm died in the night so we were late getting up — 5:30 turned into 6:30, by which time the ebb was fully underway. We had 15–20kts wind this time and the conditions in the pass were what I’d describe as rough! We crossed over to the East side seeking calmer water and snuck out very close to the reef — a little edgy, but without much more time to think about it we were already on the outside, and very glad to be there!

Kauehi

Entry: The entrance to Kauehi is on the South East. With 7 miles up to the village once inside we were very tempted to sail through this pass. The flood was still running and from what we could see it looked calm — however when we got all the way round to look in at the same angle as the current runs, it was not nearly as calm as we’d thought! This time we chose the West side and crept close to the channel marker and out of the choppy water. In the end easy, but we were very glad not to be pushed in there with full sail still up!

Exit: We timed this perfectly with the high water slack. On our way out we spotted a dive boat mooring buoy on the outside of the pass and decided to pick it up and have a look. What a good decision! We dinghied back into the pass and drift snorkelled back out on the beginning of the ebb — this was one of the best snorkels we’ve had anywhere. The coral was amazing, and we saw a huge variety of fish, including some larger pelagics on the outside wall as it drops off into the abyss.

Fakarava

Entry: Having taken the extra time to snorkel on the way out of Kauehi we were a little pressed for time, and the wind unkindly dropped on us — so we arrived late. We were bang on max flood, and the sun set as we were coming in. The pass itself was fine; fast but smooth incoming current. The problem was that we were going to have to anchor without any visibility of what we were dropping onto. The anchor held fine, and left it to the morning to untangle the inevitable mess — with Jamie in the water directing, the children did an amazing job helping Sophie lift the anchor. We had almost been unbelievably lucky; we missed an enormous sand patch by only 5m.

Exit: Finally another textbook passage — this one was our easiest. We held on while a couple of boats went out on a rough ebb, then hoisted sails before dropping the buoy. As we did so 4 ribs turned up at the pass — tour boats bringing divers — a sure sign we had timed it perfectly! We sailed out of the middle with no current or waves to speak of. A good one to finish on!

We are headed to a false pass next, on the West of Toau. After that we might stop at Makatea on our way to Tahiti — Makatea is different again, a high island with three buoys to hold you off the steep cliffs. So I think we have to hope one of them is free when we turn up!

Sad times with our visit to these wonderful atolls coming to an end. We’ve had an amazing time everywhere, and all have very special memories from each of the atolls.
entry.summary: Coming out of the North pass at Fakarava, it felt like time to write down our experience of the various atoll passes before we forget. Some were textbook, some were anything but.

entry.title: Fakarava (aka “Sharkarava”)
entry.content:
Zipper in Fakarava, including the South Pass — sharks, sharks and more sharks!

Arrival, Anchoring and Re-Anchoring

We timed our entry into the south pass from Kauehi based on the forecasted wind. Of course the wind dropped on our way and we arrived at the Fakarava South pass at max current with the sun beginning to set. Although this was very picturesque, it was far from ideal for getting in and anchored safely!

The pass looked relatively benign, so we decided to go through rather than wait all night outside. Jamie helmed through this one — it’s long, narrow and very shallow if you choose to take the shortcut. In the event the pass was fine, but we overshot the biggest sandy patch around by about 5 metres. Damn, so close!

Jamie jumped in the water and with our trusty anchor team of Isla and Angus ready on the bow, Sophie took the helm. With Jamie directing operations in the water we managed to untangle ourselves and reset the anchor in the sandy patch. Phew!

Snorkelling the South Pass

Then it was time to explore. The coral was wonderful and the fish were plentiful and huge! We also spotted a few sharks hanging out — we’ve heard they like it here.

Back to the boat for lunch before returning to the pass for the afternoon tide. We drifted out all the way from the coral garden, grabbed a handily placed buoy to hang out and relax for half an hour, then jumped back in to drift back into the pass on the incoming current. Amazing — a fantastic snorkel for everyone with sharks everywhere. The water was pristine, if a little chilly — we seem to have become too acclimatised to the tropics!

Diving the South Pass

We obviously wanted to dive the South pass. We decided we had to join a guided dive — we weren’t going to leave without diving the pass!

Sophie went on one day leaving Jamie to ponder the problem of the broken gear cable — with a promise that he could dive the next day if he managed to create a usable workaround. Sophie had two amazing dives, and on returning was impressed to find the children doing school work and Jamie making progress in the engine room. A jury system manually rigged up, sufficient to get us to Tahiti — Jamie was allowed his dive the next day!

The company we dived with was Enata Diving, and they really were excellent. Each was a two-dive trip, different from each other — catching the end of the ebb and then the start of the flood. Going early also meant there were no other tour boats out, so we had the pass to our small group of 5 each day.

We wouldn’t have found the “shark television” spot if we’d dived on our own. This is a cave/overhang where we just lay flat and held on for ~10 minutes watching literally hundreds of sharks right in front of us. There were over 500 shark in the pass both days.

Rotoava and the North Pass

After Jamie got back from his dive we headed up to the North, arriving in time to see where we were anchoring and get settled for the night. We had a gorgeous sail through the atoll, managing to avoid bommies and pearl farm floats.

The next morning we headed into town — by far the biggest town we’ve seen for a while, with the most beautifully decorated church covered with exquisite hand-made shell chandeliers and decorations. We provisioned at two shops, even managing to find eggs and some fresh fruit and vegetables. What a luxury!

On Sunday morning we saw a couple of boats head out of the pass ahead of us. It looked a bit rough so we waited an hour — and it couldn’t have been more straightforward. As we started going through, 4 dive boats also went through; a sure sign we had timed it perfectly for the slack!

Fakarava was incredible and we were all really sad to leave. Luckily Toau didn’t disappoint…
entry.summary: Sharks, sharks and more sharks. Fakarava's South Pass is one of the great dive sites in the world — and we had over 500 of them right in front of us.

entry.title: Front, Wind Shift, Anchor Drag
entry.content:
We’ve seen quite a deep low pressure developing in the forecasts for the last week or so, travelling from West to East passing well to the South of us. This has a front extending to the North which we’ve been planning to shelter from in a good anchorage — our choice was the South East corner of Fakarava.

We arrived and found our anchor spot in plenty of time, with the gribs suggesting we might see gusts of 25kts as the front came through, probably with a lot of rain:

The biggest island in this weather map is Tahiti (towards the bottom left), the island group top right is the Marquesas. We are at the green marker. Nastier colours of red and bigger arrows mean more wind, with the arrows indicating direction — we were expecting a big shift very quickly from the North all the way round to South, through the West.

The Drag

The front duly arrived at around 10am. We were anchored in 9m depth on sand, ~50m chain out, with floats at roughly 20m, 30m and 40m. This shows what happened according to our anchor alarm:

The cross in the middle is where we set the anchor. The red lines are the track of the boat. The green ring is the alarm.

#1 — Our position overnight, anchor holding well, swinging ~30 degrees to a consistent Northerly wind.

#2 — When the front hit us just before 10am. Wind swung immediately round towards the West, 20–25kts gusting 29. Anchor held nicely, swinging exactly as we would hope.

#3 — A little before 1pm the wind shifted nearly all the way round to the South, still at 25kts gusting 29. And we started to drag — the track shows we didn’t hold at this new angle at all.

This is a classic example of an anchor reset failure. The Rocna holds extremely well in one direction — we’ve been delighted with it so far — but it has a failure mode that is beginning to be more widely known. Having read about this previously we’ve been on edge every time we’ve experienced shifting winds at anchor. Until now we’ve not suffered any problem.

Re-Anchoring

We upped anchor (a little harder with 3 sets of floats to get off the chain) and got ready to reset. We got the anchor back down, further out from the lee shore, in 12m water with 60m chain and again three sets of floats.

We struggled quite a lot with keeping control, and overran the anchor chain under our port hull twice. We realised the problem — it was the bimini. In preparation for the rain we had put the sides up, and the starboard side was causing us to swing horribly.

So, another lesson learned: we won’t be putting the bimini up in strong winds at anchor in the future.

Update the following day: It turns out it wasn’t the bimini at all. The port engine was stuck in forward gear — the gear cable had sheared. So instead of idling when pulling up the chain it was driving us slowly forward. After disconnecting the cable we can now manually select forward or reverse from within the engine room. Jamie rigged up some pulleys to enable gear changes from outside — not ideal, but far better than many alternatives. Something else for the jobs list in Tahiti!
entry.summary: A 180-degree wind shift as a front went overhead, and our anchor didn't reset. A textbook example of a Rocna failure mode — and a lesson about bimini sides in strong winds.
